text: The patient is positioned supine for a laparoscopic abdominoperineal resection. The surgical team is arranged with the surgeon and cameraman on the right side and the assistant on the left. A five-port technique is utilized, including a 10mm main camera port and right-hand port, along with three 5mm ports. Monopolar cautery is used during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the sacral promontory and pulls the sigmoid colon to make the sigmoid mesentery taut. The mesentery is then opened, and the internal incision is extended down into the pelvis, stopping just at the middle of the right ureter.

The surgeon retracts the inferior mesenteric vessels anteriorly to identify the correct surgical plane situated between the vessels and the ureter, taking precautions to avoid injury to the ureter and gonadal vessels.
The surgeon continues dissection until reaching the source muscles, marking the lateral limit of the dissection. The mesenteric artery is identified at its root, skeletonized, and clipped.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to cut the inferior mesenteric artery, followed by identifying and cutting the inferior mesenteric vein using the same technique.
The surgeon identifies the holy plane located between the fascia propria and pre-sacral fascia, revealing loose areolar tissue. Dissection proceeds in this plane without bleeding.

The surgeon applies anterior traction on the rectum using a retractor, while dissecting posteriorly to reveal the pre-sacral fascia. The dissection proceeds smoothly with minimal bleeding, indicating the correct surgical plane.

The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon by incising the peritoneum lateral to it. The left ureter is identified, and dissection proceeds medial to the ureter, continuing until reaching the previously created dissection plane.
The surgeon performs left lateral dissection by retracting the rectum towards the right side and incising the peritoneum, revealing underlying anatomical structures.

Continuous traction on the rectum reveals the left lateral TMA plane between the mesorectum and pelvic fascia. The pelvic nerves, situated deep to the pelvic fascia, are not visible due to the fascia covering.
The surgeon completes the left lateral dissection and begins the right lateral dissection, ensuring to stay within the correct anatomical plane to avoid visualizing the pelvic nerves.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum on the right side, identifying the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane located between the mesorectum and the right lateral pelvic fascia. This avascular plane is dissected without bleeding, indicating correct plane adherence.

The surgeon begins anterior dissection, identifying the rectovesical pouch and incising the peritoneum to expose the seminal vesicle. The dissection proceeds between the right seminal vesicle and Denonvilliers' fascia, maintaining the avascular plane to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon performs dissection between the left seminal vesicle and the fascia, ensuring pelvic nerves are safeguarded by staying in the correct plane. The dissection continues between the prostate and tenor visor's fascia, keeping the fascia with the specimen. The lateral and posterior dissection progresses digitally until the levator muscle is visible, marking the limit of the dissection.
The fibers of the levator muscle are visible, marking the limit of the dissection in the pelvic region.

The surgeon identifies the point of colonic transection and cuts the mesocolon. The colon is transected and brought out as an end stoma. The surgical field is washed thoroughly, hemostasis is achieved, and a drain is placed. Finally, a colostomy bag is attached to the stoma.
